parts.prompt:
Calculate the number of moles of $C_2H_4$(g)
(i) that are actually produced in the experiment and measured in the gas collection tube and
(ii) that would be produced if the dehydration reaction went to completion.

scoring_points.description: Provide a correct response for part (a)(i)
Provide a correct response for part (a)(ii)

parts.prompt:
Calculate the percent yield of $C_2H_4$(g) in the experiment.
Because the dehydration reaction is not observed to occur at 298 K, the student claims that the reaction has an
equilibrium constant less than 1.00 at 298 K.

scoring_points.description: Calculate the correct value with setup and answer

required_elements: Correct mathematical setup or formula application
Correct numerical answer with appropriate sig figs and units

parts.prompt:
Do the thermodynamic data for the reaction support the student’s claim? Justify your answer, including a
calculation of DG for the reaction.

scoring_points.description: State the correct answer with a valid chemical justification

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle

parts.prompt:
The Lewis electron-dot diagram for $C_2H_4$
is shown below in the box on the left. In the box on the right,
complete the Lewis electron-dot diagram for $C_2H_5OH$ by drawing in all of the electron pairs.
What is the approximate value of the C–O–H bond angle in the ethanol molecule?

scoring_points.description: Provide a correct response for part (e)
Provide a correct response for part (f)

parts.prompt:
and unreacted $C_2H_5OH$(g) passed through the tube into the
water. The $C_2H_4$
was quantitatively collected as a gas, but the unreacted $C_2H_5OH$ was not. Explain this
observation in terms of the intermolecular forces between water and each of the two gases.

scoring_points.description: State the correct answer with a valid chemical justification

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle
